content_en:
Ar-Risalah al-Qushayriyyah achieved its goal of establishing the legitimacy of Sunni spiritual science within the mainstream of Islamic scholarship, and its influence has been enormous across the centuries and across school boundaries. It is one of the few works in Islamic literature that has been consistently cited and taught by scholars from all four major Sunni schools and from both Ash'ari-Maturidi and Athari theological backgrounds.

Al-Ghazali, writing half a century after al-Qushayri, drew heavily on the epistle in constructing the Ihya. The spiritual dictionary in ar-Risalah provided al-Ghazali with a terminological framework that he adopted and expanded, and the biographies of early masters in al-Qushayri's first section informed the way al-Ghazali presented the spiritual tradition as continuous with the most exemplary figures of early Islam. Through the Ihya's influence, al-Qushayri's framework became embedded in the standard presentation of Islamic spiritual science across the Sunni world.

Shafi'i scholars received the epistle warmly, as would be expected given al-Qushayri's own Shafi'i affiliation and his student's relationship with al-Juwayni, the great Shafi'i usuli. Maliki scholars in North Africa and the Levant also engaged with it extensively, and it became a standard reference in Maliki educational institutions across these regions. The Maliki tradition's own strong Sufi dimension — exemplified by later scholars like Ibn Ata'illah al-Iskandari and Ahmad Zarruq — developed in an intellectual environment shaped significantly by al-Qushayri's framework.

Hanbali scholars engaged with the epistle more critically, though even within the Hanbali tradition its influence is visible. Ibn Taymiyyah and Ibn al-Qayyim, while rejecting certain Sufi practices and terminological excesses, accepted the substance of many of the spiritual states and stations that al-Qushayri defined, and their own treatments of concepts like love, longing, and reliance on Allah show the influence of the terminological tradition that al-Qushayri systematized.

In the modern period, ar-Risalah has been published in numerous editions and translated into many languages. It remains the primary classical reference for students seeking to understand the historical development of Islamic spiritual science and the definitions of its key concepts, and is widely taught in traditional Islamic educational institutions.
